results.title: Assistance for Households in Recovering Fees Paid In Advance, following Sudden Closure of Student Care Centre
results.summary: The Ministry of Education has not provided new plans to assist households recovering fees from the sudden closure of Little Professors Learning Centre. Instead, the Minister directed that this matter was already addressed in the Ministry's response to Oral Parliamentary Questions Nos 3 to 6 on 2 March 2026. No additional measures, timelines, or eligibility criteria for fee recovery were stated in this specific exchange.

results.title: Active Users of LumiHealth and Reasons for Its Cessation
results.summary: The LumiHealth programme, a two-year commercial partnership between Apple and the Health Promotion Board (HPB), concluded on 31 May 2026 after engaging over 377,000 Singaporeans. While the specific number of active users as of February 2026 and the total e-vouchers issued since 2023 were not disclosed due to commercial constraints, the initiative successfully experimented with digital nudges for active lifestyles. HPB is now integrating these learnings into the broader Healthy 365 ecosystem to continue promoting healthy living, serving as the functional successor to the time-limited experiment.

results.title: Assistance for Parents and Children Affected by Childcare Centres Ceasing Operations
results.summary: An average of 70 preschools, or 3.8% of the total, ceased operations annually over the last three years. Upon closure, preschools must assist parents in securing alternative care and managing financial matters like refunding fees and deposits. The Early Childhood Development Agency (ECDA) can issue directions under the ECDC Act to ensure orderly cessation with minimal disruption and may help parents find alternative placements. For unresolved deposit or fee refund issues, parents are directed to the Consumer Association of Singapore or the Small Claims Tribunal.

results.title: A*STAR Scholarship Recipients' Research Fields and Bond Service Affected by Transitional Changes in Successive RIE Masterplans
results.summary: Approximately 80% of A*STAR scholarship graduates are employed in roles leveraging their doctoral expertise across academia and industry. The remaining 20% have pivoted to other roles, such as corporate leadership, for reasons not necessarily linked to research field deprioritisation. A*STAR facilitates redeployment through internal transfers, secondments, or moves to Institutes of Higher Learning and the private sector when required. The Minister did not specify exact numbers of scholars affected by RIE Masterplan transitions or confirm if bond release is considered for those without adjacent redeployment options.

results.title: Availability of Overseas Experience for Students of Autonomous Universities
results.summary: Autonomous universities provide overseas exposure via academic exchanges, immersions, and internships. Availability dropped sharply during the COVID-19 pandemic but has since recovered to pre-pandemic levels. The Ministry monitors these opportunities through programmes like the National University of Singapore Overseas Colleges initiative. No specific reasons for the decline were detailed beyond the pandemic impact.

results.title: Balancing Construction Timelines against Rising Costs of Raw Materials and Labour Given Accelerated Pace for Building HDB Flats
results.summary: The Minister for National Development stated that the Housing and Development Board balances accelerated Build-To-Order timelines, including Shorter Waiting Time flats, by initiating development earlier rather than incurring significantly higher construction costs. This approach maintains safety and quality while considering housing demand, land availability, and industry capacity. To enhance sector productivity, the Ministry of National Development has formed an action team with industry partners to help companies save time, costs, and manpower. The supply of flats is determined by these factors alongside the goal of launching 19,600 BTO flats in 2026.

results.title: Clarification by Minister of State for Health
results.summary: Minister of State for Health Rahayu Mahzam corrected a factual error regarding MOH's operating expenditure figures. The revised FY2025 estimate is $18.49 billion, representing a $1.63 billion (9.7%) increase over actual FY2024 expenditure, not FY2025 as previously stated. The upward trajectory continues with an estimated FY2026 operating expenditure of $20.04 billion. This clarification updates the baseline for comparing year-on-year growth in Ministry of Health spending.

results.title: Collecting Capital Gains Income Data in National Surveys to More Accurately Measure Household Market Income
results.summary: The Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Trade and Industry stated there are no plans to collect capital gains income data in national surveys. This decision aligns with international statistical standards and the practices of major national statistical agencies. Capital gains are classified as irregular changes in asset values rather than recurring income available for current consumption, and thus are not considered a component of household market income. Consequently, the Government will not adjust its measurement of household market income to include this data.

results.title: Committee of Supply Reporting Progress
results.summary: The Committee of Supply reported progress on the Estimates of Expenditure for the financial year 2026/2027. The Committee has requested leave to sit again tomorrow to continue its work. No specific policy measures, financial figures, or legislative changes were detailed in this progress report. The primary update concerns the procedural status of the Estimates review rather than new operational constraints or eligibility rules for stakeholders.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head L (Ministry of Sustainability and the Environment)
results.summary: To strengthen Singapore's food resilience against external shocks, climate change, and supply chain disruptions, the Ministry of Sustainability and the Environment is transitioning from a single-pillar approach to a four-pillar strategy under Singapore Food Story 2. This strategy integrates import diversification, local production growth, strategic stockpiles, and global partnerships to ensure reliable access to basic food needs. The 2026 Year of Climate Adaptation will focus on coastal protection and flood resilience, incorporating community input into designs like the Kranji Tidal Gatehouse viewing deck. Concurrently, the government is empowering businesses to protect premises while advancing the Beverage Container Return Scheme through Reverse Vending Machine deployment in Clementi and app accessibility improvements for seniors.

results.title: Community Support Services to Address Gaming Disorder Among Young Adults
results.summary: The Ministry of Health confirms existing community counselling programmes via TOUCH and WE CARE for gaming addiction, while the Code of Practice for Online Safety mandates app stores to implement age assurance measures by March 2026 to restrict access to age-inappropriate gaming apps. The Government will continue monitoring international developments regarding playtime limits or identity verification to assess if additional safeguards are required. Questions regarding specific regulatory mandates for game developers, such as ethical design features, real-world currency rules, and gacha mechanics, were directed to the Ministry of Digital Development and Information (MDDI) as that agency handles app design and market entry. The Ministry emphasises balancing user protection with personal autonomy for adults, focusing on upstream education and age-appropriate barriers for minors rather than strict regulation of adult users.

results.title: Commuters' Travel Patterns During Circle Line Service Adjustments for Future Planning of Rail Renewal Works
results.summary: Following the first week of Circle Line service adjustments, shuttle bus ridership settled at approximately 20,000 weekly, while precise usage of alternative rail lines remains untracked due to free interchange. Despite reduced peak frequencies for tunnel works, managed wait times and loading indicate that most commuters can still board trains, though crowding persists at busy stretches. The Acting Minister for Transport confirmed that commuter feedback will be incorporated to refine future alternative travel options for long-term service closures. No specific proportion of commuters using alternative rail lines was provided.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head O (Ministry of Health)
results.summary: The Ministry of Health proposes reducing the Head O allocation by $100 to recalibrate the S+3M programme, shifting incentives from acute episode billing toward chronic, preventive, and community-based care for conditions like diabetes, dementia, and frailty. This strategic pivot aims to support early intervention and sustain independence while addressing concerns over rising out-of-pocket costs and inequitable burdens on sandwiched families. Concurrently, MOH is integrating functional health markers into Healthier SG, expanding the Helping Hands scheme to hawker centres, and establishing a community-based caregiving corps to manage the super-aged society where one in two healthy 65-year-olds may develop severe disability. The ministry also commits to evaluating emerging evidence in precision medicine, regulating mental health professionals to reduce entry barriers, and deploying technology for home monitoring to extend healthspan and ensure meaningful outcomes for seniors.

results.title: Considerations of Ramp Installation for Corner Units of Older HDB Units and Alternative Measures to Improve Elderly Accessibility
results.summary: The Minister for National Development stated that the Housing and Development Board (HDB) provides subsidised accessibility solutions under the Enhancement for Active Seniors programme for older flats with multi-steps. Eligible options include portable ramps, customised ramps, and wheelchair lifters, subject to assessments of corridor space, fire safety, and neighbourly constraints. For cases where ramps are unsuitable, HDB will evaluate alternative measures like wheelchair lifters. Minor alterations to steps or gates may be required for installation.

results.title: Council to Provide Recommendations on Reasonable Integrated Shield Plan and Rider Premium Increases for Private Healthcare
results.summary: The Ministry of Health declined to establish a council for Integrated Shield Plan (IP) and rider premium increases, stating that such commercial decisions are best left to market forces. While acknowledging concerns about rising costs affecting seniors and middle-income households, the Minister emphasized that the Government's primary role is ensuring affordable, accessible, and quality subsidised healthcare. Instead of direct price regulation, the Ministry is focusing on consumer protection measures, including publishing information, providing guidelines for decision-making, and introducing benchmarks for private professional fees to guard against overcharging. Enforcement actions have also been taken against a minority of doctors making errant claims to improve fairness and transparency in the private healthcare sector.

results.title: Data on Crow-Related Complaints in Past Two Years and Effectiveness of Use of Bird Effigies
results.summary: The Municipal Services Office recorded 10,500 and 15,000 crow-related complaints in 2024 and 2025 respectively. The National Parks Board (NParks) confirmed bird effigies effectively disperse crows temporarily and has integrated them into a holistic management plan alongside trapping, nest removal, and upcoming shooting operations. The Minister did not specify an islandwide expansion timeline or confirm if effigies will be deployed universally, noting they complement other control measures.

results.title: Dedicated MyCareersFuture Platform for Blue-collar and Vocational Jobseekers
results.summary: MyCareersFuture currently lists vacancies for both PMET and non-PMET roles, including blue-collar and vocational positions like drivers and technicians. Jobseekers requiring additional support can access employment facilitation services through Workforce Singapore (WSG), NTUC's e2i, and appointed career matching providers Ingeus and AKG via 32 career and jobs and skills centres nationwide. Participants may also engage in career events organized by WSG, e2i, and Community Development Councils, including the Jobs Nearby @ CDC initiative designed to connect residents with local opportunities. The Minister did not confirm plans to create a separate dedicated platform for non-PMET jobseekers.

results.title: Deployment of Mobile LTA Enforcement Cameras in January 2026 and Future Rollout Plans
results.summary: In January 2026, the Land Transport Authority deployed 85 mobile enforcement cameras at various hotspots to address active mobility, bus lane, and illegal parking offences. The Authority plans to deploy additional cameras based on future enforcement needs. The Acting Minister for Transport confirmed these deployment figures and purposes, noting that future rollouts will be driven by specific operational requirements rather than a fixed schedule.

results.title: Deployment of Mobile NEA Enforcement Cameras and Future Rollout Plans
results.summary: In January 2026, the National Environment Agency deployed approximately 490 mobile enforcement cameras at public areas identified by community feedback as having persistent environmental public health issues, including high-rise littering, smoking in prohibited areas, poor refuse management, and rat infestation. The agency plans to continue expanding these deployments where necessary to improve environmental cleanliness. The response did not specify the exact future rollout timeline or total number of additional cameras planned beyond the stated intent to expand.

results.title: Employment Restrictions for Dependent Pass Holders Enrolled in Local Educational Institutions
results.summary: Dependent Pass (DP) holders enrolled in eligible local educational institutions may work part-time only if the employment is integral to their course requirements. Such work requires prior approval via a Letter of Consent. The Ministry maintains that this restriction specifically supports academic objectives rather than addressing broader foreign manpower prevalence in part-time sectors. Consequently, the policy does not currently permit general part-time employment for internship or work-study purposes outside of course mandates.

results.title: Encouraging Companies That Tap On Tax Deductions for AI Spending to Retrain Rather Than Retrench Their Workers
results.summary: Under the Enterprise Innovation Scheme, companies receiving tax deductions for AI spending face no workforce-related requirements or reporting obligations regarding headcount or wage impacts. While the Government does not mandate prioritizing retraining over retrenchment for these specific tax incentives, separate initiatives like the Enterprise Workforce Transformation Package support reskilling and role redesign with specific objectives. Consequently, the AI transformation tax scheme remains free of operational constraints on workforce metrics, whereas other grants impose distinct performance conditions.

results.title: Enhancing Public Education on Right-sizing Integrated Shield Plan Coverage
results.summary: In 2025, the Ministry of Health launched a public education campaign to encourage appropriate sizing of Integrated Shield Plan coverage and highlighted the distortive effects of IP riders. MOH publishes comparative data on benefits, features, indicative lifetime premiums, and service indicators, while the Health Insurance Planner tool assists consumers in making informed decisions. Regarding insurer profitability, aggregate gross premiums and claims data are publicly available, revealing that most insurers barely break even or incur losses on their health portfolios. The Minister noted that the distortion caused by IP riders benefits no one.

results.title: Ensuring Quality and Continuity of Care and Role of Pharmacists in Light of Increased Use of Telemedicine and Third-Party Medication Delivery Platforms
results.summary: The Ministry has instituted safeguards to regulate telemedicine and third-party medication delivery, ensuring care quality remains uncompromised despite decentralised dispensing. All telemedicine service providers must employ licensed doctors, while retail pharmacies operating under qualified pharmacist oversight must comply with the Singapore Standard Guideline for the supply and delivery of medication (SS 644: 2025). Additionally, registered medical practitioners must adhere to the Singapore Medical Council's Ethical Code and Guidelines, and pharmacists must follow the Singapore Pharmacy Council's Code of Conduct. Patients are required to receive counselling on proper medication use regardless of the care setting.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head W (Ministry of Transport)
results.summary: The Ministry of Transport anchors its strategy on three principles: being globally oriented, future-focused, and people-centric, while addressing infrastructure timelines and operational constraints. Key projects like T5 and Tuas Port are progressing with completion targeted for the mid-2030s, adding approximately 50 million annual passenger capacity and four new berths respectively. To mitigate driver shortages, autonomous bus trials will commence on routes 191 and 400 in the second half of this year, alongside a commitment to increase COE supply by 20,000 units and rebalance categories. The government will also enforce a ban on caged lorries for worker transport from 1 January 2027, encouraging companies to adopt safer canopy alternatives. Furthermore, LTA has launched a nationwide consultation to refresh the Land Transport Master Plan 2040, prioritizing connectivity, comfort, and cost-effectiveness while exploring non-pricing solutions for congestion.

results.title: Expansion of Green Mark Scheme with Corresponding Workforce Capabilities and Ensuring Integration of Sustainability Competencies
results.summary: The Building and Construction Authority's Green Mark scheme, launched in 2005, now covers over 2,500 certified projects. To align workforce capabilities with expanded sustainability standards, the Skills Framework for Built Environment positions sustainability as a core competency for architects, engineers, quantity surveyors, and facility management professionals rather than a separate track. The BCA collaborates with training providers, including Singapore Polytechnic, to update curricula for new entrants and practitioners, ensuring courses reflect the latest scheme requirements. This integrated approach ensures sustainability competencies are embedded directly into core built environment roles.

results.title: Fires Involving Blue Recycling Bins
results.summary: Over the past three years, an average of 35 fires annually involved blue recycling bins, likely caused by improper disposal of fire hazards like batteries and lit cigarettes. The National Environment Agency (NEA) addresses this risk through the annual Recycle Right Campaign, which educates the public to dispose of used batteries exclusively in e-waste bins and collection points. Residents are instructed to use blue bins solely for paper, cardboard, plastics, metals, and glass items to minimize fire incidents.

results.title: GST Relief Mechanisms for Patients Unable to Obtain Essential Medication from Public Healthcare Institutions Due to Systemic Availability Constraints
results.summary: The Minister clarified that the Government fully absorbs GST for subsidised patients obtaining medications at Public Healthcare Institutions (PHIs). If a specific medication is unavailable at one PHI, the institution will check availability at others or exercise discretion to procure it privately or assess alternative treatments. Consequently, subsidised patients are not required to obtain medically indicated medications from private sources in the vast majority of cases, thereby mitigating GST burdens arising from systemic availability constraints.

results.title: Green and Sustainability Linked Loans Originated by Financial Institutions in Singapore Offered to New Corporate and SMEs in Past Five Years
results.summary: Between 2020 and 2024, Singapore companies took up 261 green, social, sustainability, and sustainability-linked loans totaling S$137.3 billion. The Monetary Authority of Singapore does not track or provide a breakdown of these loans by borrower size, including small- and medium-sized enterprises. Consequently, the specific proportion of lending to new corporate and SMEs in each year remains unavailable.

results.title: HDB Animal Ownership Regulations, Breakdown of Pet-related Complaints in Last Five Years and Enforcement Measures to Address Pet-related Concerns
results.summary: HDB permits residents to keep up to two cats and one approved dog, alongside small pets like birds provided they cause no nuisance. Between 2021 and 2025, HDB averaged 4,000 annual pet-related feedbacks, with 47% concerning dogs, 31% cats, and 22% birds; in 2025, 20% involved excessive pet numbers, a metric not tracked previously. For nuisance cases, HDB advises owners to mitigate issues, potentially utilizing AVS-accredited dog trainers, while disputes are resolved through amicable discussion or community mediation. Recalcitrant cases may result in re-homing assistance from animal welfare groups. To promote responsible ownership, NParks mandates a free online course for first-time cat or dog licence applicants and collaborates with stakeholders on public education events.

results.title: Impact of Multiple Mounted Mobile Devices or Digital Screens on Driving Performance
results.summary: The Ministry of Home Affairs and Traffic Police have not commissioned studies on the impact of multiple mounted mobile devices or digital screens on driving performance and have no plans to review current regulations. The government maintains that the key issue is the wrongful use of devices while driving rather than their installation, as disallowing mounting could encourage drivers to hold devices, posing a graver risk. Singapore's existing framework under section 65B(1) of the Road Traffic Act prohibits holding and using mobile communication devices while a vehicle is in motion without requiring proof of harm. First-time offenders face fines up to $1,000 or imprisonment up to six months, while repeat offenders face fines up to $2,000 or imprisonment up to 12 months, alongside 12 demerit points. Rule 126 further prohibits installing visual display units that pose a safety hazard or cause distraction.

results.title: Impact of PARF Reduction on New Car Prices, Depreciation Rates and COE Renewal Behaviour
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Transport announced that the PARF rebate cap was reduced from $60,000 to $30,000 in Budget 2026, as the original objective of encouraging early vehicle renewal is less relevant with rising electric vehicle adoption. Changes to vehicle taxes and rebates apply immediately at the point of registration rather than sale, ensuring equal treatment for all bidders in a specific Certificate of Entitlement exercise. Fewer than 2,000 buyers with committed purchases, mostly of higher-end internal combustion engine cars, are estimated to be affected. Buyers with existing agreements who have not yet secured a COE are advised to discuss mutually acceptable arrangements with their dealers, as some dealers have already made alternative provisions. The Ministry maintains a balance between ownership and usage controls, with future adjustments to vehicle quotas and policies dependent on outcomes from usage tools like ERP 2.0.

results.title: Implementation of Fixed Book Price Scheme for Sing Lit Books and Books Written by Singaporean Authors
results.summary: The Ministry of Culture, Community and Youth declined to implement a Fixed Book Price scheme or introduce minimum pricing for Singapore Literature, citing mixed international views on price competition and potential affordability impacts on lower-income citizens. Instead, the Ministry will expand the SG Culture Pass scheme to include local literary works starting 1 March 2026 to enhance accessibility. This targeted approach aims to promote Sing Lit through grants and subsidies rather than price controls, acknowledging the open digital market where readers access books via international e-commerce channels. The decision reflects a preference for direct support measures over rigid pricing mechanisms.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head T (Ministry of National Development)
results.summary: The Ministry of National Development (MND) will sustain a robust housing supply, maintaining that at least 90% of new 4-room and larger BTO flats are reserved for first-timer families to ensure affordability. Regarding rental transparency, HDB will publish stall rent data for coffee shops but clarified that shop rentals operate under distinct market contracts unlike public transport fares. On housing accessibility, the 15-month resale wait-out period will be monitored rather than removed immediately, while Town Councils will trial half-height netting solutions for pest control before wider rollout. MND also committed to redesigning older estates like Toa Payoh as Age Well Neighbourhoods to support seniors aging in place, alongside plans to expand public rental housing and review income ceilings for future eligibility.

results.title: Increasing EDG Support Level to Encourage Greater Participation in Productivity and Innovation Projects
results.summary: The $0.62 billion projected underspending in FY2025 development expenditure for the Ministry of Trade and Industry is not due to a reduction in the Enterprise Development Grant (EDG) support level, which has remained unchanged since April 2023. Instead, the reduction reflects delayed reviews for larger investment projects, with disbursements rescheduled to 2026. At Budget 2026, the Prime Minister and Minister for Finance announced enhancements to internationalisation grant schemes, increasing support for local SMEs to up to 70% of eligible costs from 50%, and for local non-SMEs to up to 50% from 30%. The Government remains committed to supporting enterprise growth amid economic volatility.

results.title: Intended Role of Community Mental Health Services within Singapore’s Care Continuum
results.summary: Community mental health services in Singapore serve a dual role as first-contact screening points and post-hospital follow-up care, delivered by Community Outreach Teams (CREST) for basic support and Community Intervention Teams (COMIT) for moderate psychosocial needs. The Ministry of Health and Agency for Integrated Care monitor clinical outcomes, service utilisation, and referral sources to guide ongoing reviews and resourcing decisions. To address potential resourcing gaps, the National Mental Health Office collaborates with agencies and community partners to identify needs through structured meetings, deploying measures such as training resources and pocket guides to ensure care providers are not overburdened while maintaining service quality.

results.title: Limits of Current Funding Model for Disability Services Tagged to Organisation Rather Than Beneficiary
results.summary: The Minister for Social and Family Development clarified that the current funding model uses enrolment-based subsidies that follow the beneficiary, allowing persons with disabilities to access services from different appointed providers without bearing full costs. While portable funding is not adopted, the Ministry cites risks of suboptimal service provision and cost escalation if providers compete solely on market forces without professional guidance. Consequently, the sector relies on a regulated pool of appointed providers subject to audits and standards, with SG Enable assisting families in navigating appropriate services. The approach prioritizes sustainable costs and equitable access, particularly for those with complex needs, over unrestricted market competition.

results.title: Metrics to Assess Effectiveness of UPLIFT Enhanced School Resourcing and GEAR-UP Programmes and Results for Past Five Years
results.summary: The Ministry of Education rolled out the UPLIFT Enhanced School Resourcing (UESR) and GEAR-UP programmes across phases since 2019 to support students from disadvantaged backgrounds. By the end of 2025, UESR provided resources to 100 schools while GEAR-UP operated in 120 secondary schools, collectively benefiting over 11,000 students annually. Approximately 30% of target students with prior absenteeism issues achieved regular attendance. From 2026, these initiatives are being folded into a new enhanced support package for schools, as announced during this year's Committee of Supply debate. Specific metrics for the past five years were not provided in the response.

results.title: Monitoring Industry-led Guideline of Proper Distribution of Property Flyers at HDB Estates
results.summary: An industry-led memorandum of understanding between the Singapore Institute of Estate Agents and five major estate agents, supported by the Council for Estate Agencies, mandates that property flyers distributed in HDB estates must not be visible to the public other than the intended recipient. Effective 1 April 2026, a "three strikes" penalty system will apply to errant agents, with the most severe sanction being a six-month suspension from distributing flyers. The Council for Estate Agencies will maintain a central repository of breaches and monitor resident feedback after the framework commences. Should self-regulation prove insufficient, the Ministry will assess whether legislative or regulatory measures are required based on holistic evaluation of the situation.

results.title: Monitoring of EASE Programme Take-up Rate by Geography, Housing Type and Household
results.summary: The Minister for National Development confirmed that take-up rates for the direct Enhancement for Active Seniors (EASE) programme are monitored by HDB zones, flat types, and households. Data covering applications from the programme's July 2012 launch through 23 February 2026 will be shared. The response provided a specific breakdown of application numbers across these geographical and housing categories. No further details on future monitoring adjustments or specific rate figures were disclosed in the transcript.

results.title: Number of Air-conditioned Classrooms in Schools and Schools that are Most Vulnerable to Urban Heat Island Effect
results.summary: The Ministry of Education aims to provide heat-resilient learning environments through passive cooling designs for all new schools, targeting Green Mark Platinum Super Low Energy certification. By 2027, all schools will receive additional powerful fans and cool paint coatings on external facades. For classrooms requiring extended enclosure due to dust or noise, air-conditioning will be deployed. MOE is identifying schools with specific site constraints causing pervasive heat build-up and will target additional cooling measures where current passive strategies are insufficient. While a 2024 National University of Singapore study confirms cognitive performance declines above certain temperature thresholds, the Ministry will tailor cooling measures to schools experiencing greater student discomfort rather than applying uniform standards.

results.title: Outreach to Lower-income Singaporeans and Underserved Groups to Encourage Utilisation of SG Culture Pass
results.summary: The Ministry of Culture, Community and Youth (MCCY) is partnering with social and community agencies to ensure lower-income Singaporeans and underserved groups utilise the SG Culture Pass. Specific measures include organising outings with Active Ageing Centres for seniors, arranging onsite programmes for nursing home residents, and leveraging People's Association networks to bring eligible events to heartlands. Additionally, the WeCare Arts Experience fund has been expanded to allow agencies to claim costs for transport and logistics, enabling beneficiaries like low-income families to access programmes. MCCY will continue these partnerships to facilitate easier access to local arts and heritage events for all Singaporeans.

results.title: PSEA Balance Transfers to CPF Ordinary Account at Age 31 and Uptake of Subsequent Education or Reskilling
results.summary: Upon reaching age 31, remaining Post-Secondary Education Account (PSEA) balances are transferred to the CPF Ordinary Account; last year, approximately two-thirds of accounts had balances transferred. Many individuals continue pursuing education and reskilling after entering the workforce, supported by government initiatives. In 2025, around 500,000 individuals aged 31 and above participated in SkillsFuture Singapore-supported training, utilizing course fee subsidies of up to 70% and SkillsFuture Credit. Mid-career workers aged 40 and above can access the SkillsFuture Level-Up Programme for substantive skills reboot, ensuring continued access to quality training throughout their careers.

results.title: Participation Rates of Schools and Students for UPLIFT Enhanced School Resourcing and GEAR-UP Programmes in Past Five Years
results.summary: Since 2019, the Uplifting Pupils in Life and Inspiring Families Taskforce Enhanced School Resourcing (UESR) and Guiding and Empowering Students for Affiliation and Resilience to Unlock their Potential (GEAR-UP) programmes have supported 100 and 120 secondary schools respectively, collectively benefiting over 11,000 students annually. Approximately 30% of target students with prior absenteeism issues achieved regular attendance through these initiatives. From 2026, UESR and GEAR-UP will be integrated into a new enhanced support package for schools, as announced by the Ministry of Education during this year's Committee of Supply debate. Specific participation figures for each of the past five years were not provided in the response.

results.title: Philantrophic Contributions Made by Single Family Offices to Local Charitable Organisations Since 2024
results.summary: The Minister referred the Member to the Monetary Authority of Singapore's response regarding Single Family Offices' economic and social contributions, provided during the Parliament Sitting on 24 September 2025. The response addresses the tracking of philanthropic contributions made by Single Family Offices to local charitable organisations since 2024. Specific figures on the total contribution amount or reasons for non-tracking were not detailed in this transcript. The Minister directed the Member to the referenced MAS response for further details on this matter.

results.title: Plans to Manage Numbers of Private Hire Car Driver's Vocational Licences Issued
results.summary: The Minister confirmed that the number of Private Hire Car (PHC) Driver's Vocational Licences has stabilised after rising from 28,000 ten years ago to roughly 70,000 today, with no current plans to constrain issuance. This approach aims to maintain PHCs as a vital pay-per-use transport option for those unable or unwilling to own cars, complementing public transport which remains the policy focus. While the Minister acknowledged concerns about drivers losing livelihoods if numbers grow too large, he stated there are no intentions to restrict the market, noting the diverse group includes full-time professionals and those in transient employment. The government will leave driver numbers to market forces, though the Minister encouraged some drivers to consider joining the bus industry to help address public transport constraints.

results.title: Proportion of Migrant Workers Living in Purpose-built Dormitories with Access to Nearby Recreational Centres
results.summary: Approximately 22% of non-Malaysian migrant workers in the construction, marine, and process sectors reside in purpose-built dormitories within one kilometre of a recreation centre. To enhance accessibility, free shuttle bus services operate from select large dormitories and MRT stations. Operators must maintain reasonable pricing for goods and services, with funding support contingent upon meeting annual visitorship targets. These measures aim to ensure affordable access to recreational facilities for the specified workforce.

results.title: Proportion of Polyclinic Consultations Conducted via Telehealth Services
results.summary: Telehealth services accounted for approximately 2% of total polyclinic attendances in both 2024 and 2025, with usage limited to appropriate cases like test result reviews and chronic condition follow-ups rather than initial consultations. The Ministry of Health is not currently tracking specific patient satisfaction metrics for telehealth, though polyclinics gather general feedback to drive service improvements. Efforts are underway to expand telehealth usage where suitable and to assist patients with technology navigation through community partnerships.

results.title: Proposal for Higher Compensation for Local Authors and Publishers for Books Provided to NLB Libraries for Public Borrowing
results.summary: The Minister for Digital Development and Information stated that the National Library Board (NLB) supports local authors and publishers by purchasing books at fair market value and promoting Singapore Collections. She explained that paying above market prices would limit the NLB's ability to acquire sufficient titles to meet diverse user needs. Instead of higher remuneration for public borrowing, the NLB supports the local literary ecosystem through initiatives like book launches and facilitating sales at events. The Ministry will continue these broader efforts to build a vibrant reading environment in Singapore.

results.title: Providing Tracking Devices for Dementia Patients at Subsidised Rates
results.summary: The Ministry of Health does not recommend routine use of tracking devices for dementia patients due to ethical and privacy concerns. While some community trials are underway, no plan exists to provide these devices at subsidised rates as part of standard care. Eligibility for such devices remains restricted to specific trial contexts rather than general public access. The Minister clarified that any deployment requires careful consideration within a broader care plan.

results.title: Reason for Lower-than-projected Health Ministry Operating Expenditure in FY2025
results.summary: The Ministry of Health's revised FY2025 operating expenditure is $310 million lower than initial estimates, representing a 1.6% reduction against an $18.8 billion total. This variance stems from lower-than-expected cost increases at public healthcare clusters and timing differences in operational decisions, though overall spending remains on an upward trajectory with FY2026 estimates at $20.04 billion. The Minister clarified that specific operational cost details are non-public and confirmed that patient care was not compromised despite the reduction. While the Minister acknowledged concerns regarding an ageing population, she stated that healthcare services remain a priority and continue to be delivered effectively to patients.

results.title: Reason for Voluntary Approach to New Guideline on Property Flyers to HDB Homes
results.summary: The Council for Estate Agencies (CEA) implemented an industry-led memorandum of understanding (MOU) between the Singapore Institute of Estate Agents and five major estate agents to address complaints regarding property flyers left on HDB doors, effective 1 April 2026. This voluntary framework replaces statutory prohibition, requiring flyers to be distributed without public visibility and utilizing a "three strikes" penalty system where the maximum sanction is a six-month suspension. The CEA will monitor feedback post-commencement to assess compliance holistically and determine if further measures are necessary, while maintaining a central repository of breaches managed by the Singapore Institute of Estate Agents.

results.title: Reconsideration of Decision to Order Removal of Mural on 59 Arab Street
results.summary: The Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) ordered the removal of a mural at 59 Arab Street because murals are prohibited on front façades of conserved shophouses, except at Haji Lane, to prevent obscuring architectural features and altering the historic district's character. URA consulted the Kampong Gelam Alliance and the Heritage and Identity Partnership, both of whom agreed to maintain existing guidelines. The tenant has since complied and removed the mural. No reconsideration of this decision was requested or granted in the transcript.

results.title: Recorded Incidences of AI-Induced Psychosis in Past Three Years and Measures to Address AI-Related Mental Health Issues
results.summary: The Ministry for Health clarified that "AI-induced psychosis" is not an established clinical diagnosis, despite international reports on psychological disturbances linked to intensive AI use. While no clear upward trend in local cases has been observed beyond a few anecdotal instances, the Ministry is actively monitoring the potential health risks posed by AI and digital media. No specific case statistics for the past three years were provided, and the Ministry is working with relevant partners to further study and address these emerging concerns.

results.title: Regular Training in Communications and Customer Service Excellence for Frontline Public Service Officers
results.summary: The Singapore Public Service ensures consistent, high-quality citizen-centric service delivery through a three-pronged approach: strengthening officer competencies, enforcing clear standards, and redesigning processes. Competency development includes continuous learning via a Service Competency Framework, agency-specific training on policies and soft skills, and cross-agency immersion programmes. The government sets and regularly updates whole-of-Government service delivery standards for public queries and digital services. Process redesign leverages citizen feedback data to streamline services around life moments, accessible seamlessly via LifeSG. For those unable to transact digitally, ServiceSG Centres act as a safety net, providing assistance for over 600 commonly needed services from more than 25 agencies. These integrated efforts sustain continuous service quality improvements across the whole-of-Government.

results.title: Relaxing Conservation Guidelines for Painting of Murals in Heritage Districts
results.summary: The Urban Redevelopment Authority maintains prevailing conservation guidelines for murals on conserved shophouses in heritage districts like Kampong Gelam, despite opportunities for art to increase vibrancy. While murals are permitted on side walls of corner shophouses and rear boundary walls, the Authority consulted the Kampong Gelam Alliance and Heritage and Identity Partnership, who agreed to keep current restrictions. No study was conducted or released regarding the impact of relaxing these guidelines on tourism, as the existing framework balances artistic expression with heritage preservation.

results.title: Restriction Orders Issued under Protection from Scams Act 2025
results.summary: As of 15 February 2026, 12 Restriction Orders under the Protection from Scams Act 2025 are in effect, with one lifted and two ceasing after reaching statutory maximum extensions. Victims may apply to the Police to withdraw funds for legitimate purposes like daily expenses, which are processed quickly to minimise inconvenience. While nine orders remain active, the Police approved withdrawal requests for about 10 recipients, while two made no requests. The government has decided not to prioritise graduated restrictions, such as transaction limits or preserved GIRO payments, because banks have indicated that significant system changes are required to implement them.

results.title: Review Discretion Given to Supermarket Operators on Use of Funds Collected from Plastic Bag Charges
results.summary: The National Environment Agency requires supermarket operators to publish annual reports detailing disposable carrier bag supply, proceeds collected, and fund usage to ensure transparency. The majority of collected funds are channeled toward environmental or social causes, with public and NGO scrutiny encouraging judicious allocation. The government will continue monitoring operator compliance and encouraging the channeling of proceeds to these designated causes. No review of the discretion given to operators regarding fund usage was proposed or indicated in the response.

results.title: Risk Points Identified in Street Count of Rough Sleepers 2025 Report for Entry into Long-term Homelessness
results.summary: The 2025 Street Count of Rough Sleepers report identified approximately 500 individuals sleeping rough, prompting MSF to address life transitions like marital breakdown, incarceration release, and eviction as key vulnerability stressors. Upstream prevention frameworks are operationalized through cross-agency support: Family Service Centres provide counselling and financial guidance for divorcing individuals, while community partners lead coordinated reintegration efforts including case management for those released from incarceration. For those facing eviction or institutional discharge, MSF implements casework interventions, financial assistance schemes, and referrals to temporary shelter options. The Ministry continues to refine inter-agency collaborations to identify at-risk persons and families, aiming to reduce rough sleeping incidences through these targeted upstream measures.

results.title: Risk of In-flight Use of Power Banks
results.summary: CAAS aligns its regulations with International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) guidelines regarding lithium battery safety, including capacity limits and usage restrictions. While Japan has banned in-flight power bank use, CAAS currently follows ICAO standards and is preparing for upcoming ICAO guideline updates expected in the coming months. The Authority is collaborating with airlines to implement these anticipated regulatory changes. Specific thresholds for stricter restrictions will be adopted once ICAO finalizes its updates.

results.title: Safeguards to Prevent Misuse of Training Work Permit Which Imposes Lower Levy
results.summary: The Ministry has implemented specific safeguards to prevent misuse of the Training Work Permit (TWP) scheme, which offers a lower levy for short-term practical training. Eligibility is restricted to foreign trainees from related overseas companies and students fulfilling course requirements, with validity capped at six months and strict non-renewal conditions. TWP holders are subject to Dependency Ratio Ceilings and higher-skilled Work Permit levy rates, maintaining a low population of approximately 0.2% of total Work Permit holders. Employers found deploying TWP holders in non-training roles face financial penalties of up to $10,000 per worker and potential debarment from employing foreign workers.

results.title: Setting Up Helpline and Oversight for Tripartite Framework for the Prevention of Abuse and Harassment in Healthcare
results.summary: The Ministry of Health (MOH) maintains that public healthcare institutions possess internal mechanisms for immediate response, reporting, and post-incident management, noting that internal assessment is often required to handle medical confidentiality when perpetrators are patients. While the Tripartite Workgroup convenes regularly to share best practices, MOH clarifies that hospitals are best positioned to judge incidents involving mental health or lack of self-control, though concerns regarding potential conflict of interest are acknowledged. To address fairness concerns, the Minister assured that any allegation of institutional unfairness can be escalated directly to MOH for a separate independent review. The Government reiterates its zero-tolerance stance against abuse, with perpetrators reported to the Police where appropriate, but no new centralised helpline or independent oversight body is currently being established under the Tripartite Framework.

results.title: Steps to Reduce Vegetation Fires
results.summary: The Singapore Civil Defence Force (SCDF) leads an inter-agency Wildfire Task Force to proactively minimise vegetation fire risks amid climate-induced dry weather. Preventive measures include monitoring weather patterns, identifying dense vegetation hotspots, watering and trimming overgrown areas, creating fire breaks, and educating the public against discarding lighted materials. While vegetation fires have generally remained under 200 annually over the last five years, the government notes that numbers can spike to over 800 during sustained dry spells, as seen in 2019. Consequently, SCDF emphasises the need for a long-term perspective and heightened vigilance, particularly when the Meteorological Service anticipates dry weather, to implement additional proactive prevention methods.

results.title: Summonses Issued to Drivers for Using Mobile Devices While Driving in Past Five Years
results.summary: Between 2021 and 2025, approximately 2,800 motorists were issued summonses annually for using handheld mobile communication devices while driving. An offence requires the vehicle to be in motion, the device to be held in hand, and an active operation such as typing, swiping, or viewing video; using a mounted device is not an offence under this specific charge. However, drivers using mounted devices may still face liability for driving without due care, reasonable consideration, or for reckless and dangerous driving depending on case facts.

results.title: Tracking Environmental Impact of AI Usage
results.summary: The Minister confirmed Singapore lacks a pre-determined upper limit for data centre capacity but adopts a calibrated growth approach within resource constraints and long-term environmental commitments. Resource footprints are monitored using internationally recognised metrics like power and water usage effectiveness, which inform assessments for new capacity allocated via the competitive Call for Application mechanism. Applicants must demonstrate best-in-class resource efficiency, and the upcoming Digital Infrastructure Act aims to further uplift energy efficiency and sustainability. Specific methodologies and findings from the related Parliamentary Question by Ms Elysa Chen were referenced but not detailed in this response.

results.title: Tracking Productivity Growth Rather Than Adoption Rates to Measure Success of AI Strategy
results.summary: The Government tracks AI strategy success via national productivity impact alongside adoption rates. Pre-approved, market-tested AI solutions and vendors are available to improve productivity, while customised solutions require grant recipients to meet committed milestones before further disbursement. Co-payment requirements ensure recipient companies maintain a stake in project success. The Ministry will study leveraging these assessments to refine grant conditions, balancing effectiveness with avoiding onerous rules that deter genuine efforts to improve.

results.title: Utilisation Rate of Government Co-Matching Child Development Accounts across Household Income and Caregiving Arrangements
results.summary: The Ministry of Social and Family Development (MSF) studied Child Development Account (CDA) utilisation rates for birth cohorts from 2008 to 2011 across income bands. While most parents maximised Government co-matching for the first two children, lower-income families saved less than caps from the third child onwards, resulting in lower account balances at closure. The study noted that income data relied on parental declarations at application and excluded cohorts eligible for later schemes like the First Step Grant. MSF has not conducted a study on utilisation rate differences across caregiving circumstances.
